summary: Task 5 infrastructure changes (version catalog, build script, manifest, privacy XML) are correct, build-verified (assembleDebug SUCCESS + aapt confirmation), and align with project conventions and tech-spec decisions D8/D11/D18/D25/D26. No critical or major findings. A handful of minor suggestions: data-extraction-rules uses non-idiomatic path="." form (matches spec hint, but `<exclude domain="root" />` is the Android-canonical exclude-all), redundant `android:fullBackupContent="false"` kept alongside `allowBackup="false"`, and a recommendation to pin compose-richtext to a stable version once one ships. All alias naming follows the established kebab-case convention; no duplicate deps; `lifecycle-runtime-compose` was already present in Phase 1 and correctly NOT re-added.

findings.issue: Both `<exclude>` elements carry `path="."`. Android's canonical exclude-all form for a domain is simply `<exclude domain="root" />` (no `path` attribute). The `path` attribute is designed to point at a specific relative sub-file/sub-folder; `path="."` is not documented in the Android reference examples and its semantics (current directory of the domain root) are identical to omitting it. The build passes because the manifest-merger and PackageManager accept it, but it adds noise that a future reader may misread as 'exclude only the dotfile entry'.
findings.suggestion: Replace both `<exclude domain="root" path="." />` with `<exclude domain="root" />`. If you want to stay byte-for-byte with the tech-spec D26 hint, update D26 in the tech-spec in the same sweep so the implementation and the spec agree on the idiomatic form. Verification stays the same: `aapt dump xmltree ... data_extraction_rules.xml` will still show the exclude elements.

findings.issue: `android:fullBackupContent="false"` is kept on `<application>` alongside `android:allowBackup="false"` and `android:dataExtractionRules="@xml/data_extraction_rules"`. When `allowBackup="false"`, `fullBackupContent` is ignored by the platform (there is no backup to shape), and on Android 12+ (minSdk=31 here), `dataExtractionRules` supersedes `fullBackupContent` for the legacy cloud-backup path anyway. The redundant attribute does not break anything, but it dilutes the signal of 'one source of truth for privacy config'.
findings.suggestion: Drop `android:fullBackupContent="false"` â€” `allowBackup="false"` + `dataExtractionRules="@xml/data_extraction_rules"` on minSdk 31 is the complete and minimal set. If you prefer to keep it as defense-in-depth, add a one-line XML comment above the `<application>` block explaining why all three coexist so future maintainers don't remove the 'wrong' one.

findings.issue: `composeRichtext = "1.0.0-alpha02"` is an alpha version. Phase-2 tech-spec D8 already acknowledges this, and the build passes with Compose BOM 2026.03.00, but the alpha status means: (a) no API stability guarantees across alpha02 â†’ beta/stable, (b) any Compose BOM bump could silently break binary compatibility, (c) Renovate/Dependabot will not auto-suggest upgrades within the alpha channel by default. There is no conflict observed in this commit â€” the richtext artifacts are independent of BOM-managed Compose groups â€” but the risk is real for Phase 2 lifetime.
findings.suggestion: No immediate change. Add a TODO comment in `libs.versions.toml` next to `composeRichtext` (e.g. `# alpha â€” watch https://github.com/halilozercan/compose-richtext/releases; upgrade when a stable/beta lands`). When Phase 2 tech-spec is finalized, add a decision note (D8 or a follow-up) that the richtext version is intentionally pinned and must be re-evaluated before Phase 5 release.

findings.issue: New dependency block ordering is reasonable (CameraX group, then richtext group, separated by a blank line), but the block comes after the `ksp(libs.hilt.android.compiler)` line. Convention across the repo (see the lifecycle block on lines 47-49 and the Compose block on lines 52-59) is 'implementation groups first, then annotation-processor / ksp last'. Inserting new `implementation(...)` lines after `ksp(...)` technically works (Gradle does not care about order within a `dependencies {}` block) but breaks the visual convention.
findings.suggestion: Move the new CameraX + richtext blocks before the Hilt section, or at minimum insert them above the `ksp(libs.hilt.android.compiler)` line. Concretely, relocate lines 65-71 to between current lines 59 and 61 so the file reads: [core modules] â†’ [androidx base] â†’ [Compose BOM + UI] â†’ [CameraX] â†’ [richtext] â†’ [Hilt impl + ksp]. Optional: add brief section comments (`// CameraX`, `// compose-richtext`) for the two new groups to match the implicit grouping the rest of the file already uses.

findings.issue: Two versions (`camerax`, `composeRichtext`) are appended at the bottom of the `[versions]` block, and the corresponding six library aliases are appended at the bottom of `[libraries]`. Current ordering across the catalog is chronological (by when an entry was added), not alphabetical or grouped-by-domain. This is consistent with the rest of the file, so it is not a regression â€” but as the catalog grows past ~30 libraries it becomes harder to locate an entry at a glance.
findings.suggestion: Non-blocking for this task. Consider a standalone chore to alphabetize both `[versions]` and `[libraries]` sections, or introduce banner comments grouping by domain (`# --- AndroidX core ---`, `# --- Compose ---`, `# --- Camera ---`, `# --- Markdown ---`, `# --- Testing ---`). Current ordering is internally consistent, so this is purely ergonomic.

findings.issue: New `<uses-feature android:name="android.hardware.camera" android:required="false" />` correctly lives at top-level (between `<uses-permission>` lines and `<application>`) and its `required="false"` matches D11 + tech-spec rationale (not excluding camera-less devices from Play). `<uses-feature android:name="android.hardware.microphone" android:required="false" />` is NOT declared alongside `RECORD_AUDIO`. Play Console filters devices on both permissions AND implicit hardware features: `RECORD_AUDIO` on its own implies `android.hardware.microphone` (required=true by default unless declared). For a device-agnostic app this is usually the intent on phones, but a tablet/desktop-Android target might be filtered out if no microphone hardware is reported.
findings.suggestion: If targeting the full Play Store breadth (non-phone form factors), add `<uses-feature android:name="android.hardware.microphone" android:required="false" />` next to the camera uses-feature. If the app is explicitly phone-only (Honor 200 is the target device and the user-spec treats phones as the universe), the implicit `required=true` is the correct, even slightly preferable, default â€” in that case ignore this finding. Recommend adding a one-liner to tech-spec D11 clarifying the intended device scope.

findings.issue: patterns.md Â§ Library-module manifest hygiene requires `:core-runtime`'s manifest to declare every permission the library's code touches, so library-scope lint does not fire `MissingPermission`. Task 5 adds CameraX artifacts to `:app` only (CameraX consumers will live in `:app/ui`), and RECORD_AUDIO is used by UI code (audio-record bottom sheet) â€” both flows are in `:app`, so the `:core-runtime` manifest does NOT need CAMERA / RECORD_AUDIO added here. This is NOT a finding against Task 5; it is a reminder for whoever lands the corresponding implementation tasks â€” if any `:core-runtime` code ever calls `Camera` or `AudioRecord` APIs (e.g., if an audio transcoder were pushed into core), the corresponding `<uses-permission>` must be mirrored in `core-runtime/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml` at that time.
findings.suggestion: No change to Task 5. Record this as a checklist item in the review/decision log for the tasks that actually wire camera capture and audio recording: 'Confirm all camera/audio API call sites live in :app; if any land in :core-runtime, mirror the permission in core-runtime/AndroidManifest.xml per patterns.md library-manifest hygiene rule.'
